title: Saag paneer
subtitle: Cubes of paneer in a smooth spinach curry, finished with cream and fenugreek.
excerpt: Spinach cooked down to a deep green purée, spiced with cumin, coriander, ginger and a generous pinch of dried fenugreek, then folded around pan-fried cubes of paneer. Forty-five minutes, vegetarian, and as a useful introduction to the curry-house dairy curries — the spice base teaches the same grammar as butter chicken and tikka masala without the meat.
sourceNotes:
Colonel A. R. Kenney-Herbert ('Wyvern'), Culinary Jottings for Madras (1878) — Project Gutenberg. Wyvern documented the spiced spinach preparations of the Anglo-Indian table; though paneer itself rarely appeared in his English-language writing, the spinach base he described (greens cooked down with onion, ginger, and ground spice) is the conceptual ancestor of the modern saag paneer.

The pairing of paneer cubes with a spinach base is older as a North Indian and Punjabi home dish; the modern British curry-house version sits between the traditional palak paneer (smooth spinach with less cream) and the post-war restaurant style with a richer dairy finish. Proportions here follow the restaurant-tradition middle ground.

recipe.freezeNotes: The spinach base freezes for three months in a sealed container. Pan-fried paneer doesn't freeze well — the texture goes spongy — so freeze the sauce alone and fry fresh paneer when you reheat. Defrost overnight in the fridge.
recipe.batchNotes: Double the sauce in a wide saucepan; the paneer fries best in a separate pan in single layers and reheats in the warm sauce.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The spinach base can be made a day ahead and reheated; the flavour rounds overnight. Fry the paneer fresh on the day of serving.

recipeTools.notes: Wide 28 cm pan for the sauce.
For a smooth spinach base. A stick blender in the saucepan works too.

glossaryTerms.definition: Anglicised Punjabi word for greens, broadly. In British-Indian menus saag means spinach by default, sometimes blended with mustard greens, fenugreek leaves or other leafy greens. Different from palak, which specifically means spinach in Hindi and Urdu, though the dishes overlap. Saag preparations are cooked down to a purée rather than served as wilted leaves.

Fresh Indian cheese made by curdling milk with lemon juice or vinegar, draining, and pressing into a firm cake. It doesn't melt: cubes hold their shape in a hot pan and in a finished curry. Most British supermarkets stock blocks of paneer in the chilled section; making it at home from whole milk and lemon juice takes about half an hour.

content.text: Saag paneer is a spinach curry, smooth and dark green, with cubes of fried paneer cheese folded through at the end. Forty-five minutes start to finish, vegetarian, and warmer than its short ingredient list suggests. The spinach reduces dramatically: 500 g of fresh leaves cooks down to roughly half a teacup of purée. The spice base provides the warmth that the spinach alone wouldn't carry.
Two preparations happen in parallel. The
paneer
is cubed and pan-fried until golden on the edges; this step adds texture and stops the cheese from disintegrating into the sauce. In a separate pan, the
saag

base builds: onion sweated soft, ginger and garlic paste, ground spices bloomed in fat, spinach added in handfuls until it wilts, then the whole thing blitzed smooth. The fried paneer rejoins at the very end with a splash of cream.

Three working notes. First, fresh spinach gives a brighter flavour than frozen, but frozen works in a hurry; squeeze it dry before adding. Second, the paneer wants real colour on at least two faces of each cube — golden, not just warm. Third, the dried fenugreek leaves added at the end are the ingredient that makes the dish taste like a restaurant curry rather than a tame vegetable side; skip them at your peril.

attrs.title: Why fry the paneer first
attrs.body: Untreated paneer breaks down in a hot sauce: the cubes soften, blur at the edges, and lose their shape. A short fry in hot oil sets a thin golden crust on each face. The crust holds the cube together when it meets the sauce, gives a textural contrast against the smooth purée, and adds toasty notes that raw paneer doesn't carry. Three minutes of attentive cooking; well worth it.

content.text: What you need

items.prepNote: fresh; baby leaf or large leaf both work
juice of half a lemon, to finish

content.text: Two pans go at once: a small frying pan for the paneer cubes and a wider sauté pan for the saag base. A microplane handles ginger and garlic in under a minute. A stick blender in the saucepan or a jug blender on a brief pulse finishes the spinach smooth — a chunky version works too, on the rustic side.
Method
Fry the paneer

Heat {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a frying pan over a medium-high heat. Cut {{paneer}} of paneer into 2 cm cubes and lay them out in a single layer. Cook for two minutes a side until two or three faces of each cube are deep golden. Tip onto kitchen paper. The cubes will firm up as they cool.
Start the saag base

Melt {{ghee}} of ghee in a wide sauté pan over a medium heat. Add {{cumin-seeds}} of cumin seeds — they should sizzle on contact and start popping within five seconds. Let them go for fifteen seconds, then tip in {{onion}} finely chopped onion and {{chilli-green}} chopped green chilli. Cook for eight minutes, stirring every couple of minutes, until the onion is soft and starting to colour at the edges.
Bloom the spices

Stir in {{ginger-root}} of grated ginger and {{garlic}} of grated garlic; cook for a minute. Add {{coriander-ground}} of ground coriander and {{turmeric}} of turmeric. Stir for thirty seconds in the hot fat: the smell should turn toasty, the spices should look glossy, and the oil should start pulling away at the edges of the paste. A splash of water saves it if anything sticks.
Wilt in the spinach

Add {{spinach}} of spinach a handful at a time. Stir each handful into the pan and let it wilt before adding the next; the volume will look enormous at first and reduce dramatically. Once all the spinach is in, add {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and 50 ml of water. Cover, lower the heat, and cook for ten minutes. The mixture should look dark green and a little soupy.
Blitz smooth

Take the pan off the heat. Blitz the spinach mixture with a stick blender until smooth — or tip into a jug blender, pulse a few times, and return to the pan. A chunky version works too if you prefer texture; a smooth purée is the restaurant-tradition default and the cream sits more evenly in it.
Finish with cream and fenugreek

Return the pan to a low heat. Stir in {{double-cream}} of double cream. Crumble in {{fenugreek-leaves}} of dried fenugreek leaves between your fingers — the aroma lift here is unmistakable. Stir in {{garam-masala}} of garam masala. Taste and adjust with salt.
Fold in the paneer

Slide the fried paneer cubes into the sauce. Turn them gently in the warm spinach to coat. Warm through for two minutes, no more, so the cubes keep their crust. Squeeze over a little lemon juice if the dish needs lifting. Serve with basmati rice or naan, and a bowl of plain yoghurt on the side.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Saag paneer goes wrong at the same handful of places: spinach that tastes raw, paneer that falls apart, a finished dish that reads as bland or bitter.

items.symptom: Spinach tastes raw and grassy, not deep.
items.cause: Spinach not cooked long enough after wilting.
items.fix: Wilting alone is not enough; the spinach wants ten minutes of covered cooking after that to lose its raw edge. If the dish still tastes grassy, return it to the heat covered for another five minutes; the colour will deepen and the flavour will round out.

items.cause: Cubes not fried first, or stirred too aggressively at the end.
items.fix: Fry the paneer until at least two faces of each cube are deep golden; the crust holds the cube together. Fold the cubes through the sauce with a gentle hand and only at the end; never simmer paneer in a sauce for longer than two minutes once the cubes are in.

items.cause: Spices not bloomed, or fenugreek leaves left out.
items.fix: The thirty-second cook of the ground spices in the hot ghee is the step that cooks out the raw edge. The dried fenugreek leaves at the finish add the takeaway-style aroma; the dish reads as flat without them. Crumble them between your fingers as you add to release the oils.

items.cause: Burnt cumin seeds at the start, or burnt spices at the bloom.
items.fix: The cumin seeds want fifteen seconds in the hot fat, not thirty. The ground spices want thirty seconds, not a minute. If they go black rather than golden-brown they'll throw a bitter note through the whole sauce; in that case it's better to start the spice base again.

items.cause: Not enough water during the spinach simmer, or blitzed too long.
items.fix: Splash in another 50 ml of water after the cream and warm through. The blitz should pulse to smooth, not whip; over-blending breaks the spinach down too far and the sauce reads gluey rather than silky.

content.text: Variations

Palak paneer leaves the cream out and uses only spinach as the green; the result is sharper and brighter. Saag aloo replaces the paneer with par-boiled potato cubes for a vegan dinner. Add a handful of fresh fenugreek leaves (methi) along with the spinach for a deeper flavour. For a richer version, replace half the cream with full-fat yoghurt and stir it in at the very end, off the heat, to avoid splitting.
Make ahead, freezing, leftovers

The spinach base can be made a day ahead and reheated; the flavours round overnight. Fry the paneer fresh on the day of serving — fried cubes lose their crust in the fridge. The spinach base alone freezes for three months in a sealed container; defrost overnight in the fridge and fry fresh paneer to fold in. Cold leftovers reheat gently in a covered pan with a splash of water.
Where this dish lives

Saag paneer sits at a crossroads between Punjabi home cooking and the British-Indian restaurant tradition. Spinach with cubes of fresh cheese is older as a North Indian and Punjabi village dish — paneer is one of the few cheeses traditional Indian dairying produces, and pairing it with whatever green was in season is centuries old. The British curry-house version codified the dish in the post-war years: smooth spinach base, fried paneer cubes, a finish of cream and fenugreek that signals the restaurant style.

At a British family table it works as a vegetarian main with rice and naan, or as a side beside a lamb or chicken curry. It is also the gateway curry for many home cooks: the grammar of fried spice base, aromatic paste, wilted vegetable, finished with cream and fenugreek, is the same grammar that runs through butter chicken, tikka masala, korma, and the rest of the curry-house dairy curries. Once a cook can produce a smooth saag paneer, the others become a matter of swapping the protein and adjusting the spice ratio.
